players.name: Nikola Vasilj
players.bio: Mostar-born shot-stopper who became Bosnia's first choice between the sticks at FC St. Pauli, where his performances in the Bundesliga earned him cult status. A commanding presence with sharp reflexes, Vasilj is the Dragon's last line of defence at his first World Cup.
players.facts: Vasilj kept goal for St. Pauli during their emotional Bundesliga promotion — a club with one of football's most passionate fanbases.

players.name: Nihad Mujakić
players.bio: Sarajevo-born centre-back who found his footing in Turkish football with Gaziantep. Powerful in the air and reads the game well for his age. With just 12 caps, the World Cup stage offers Mujkić a chance to announce himself to a global audience.
players.facts: Mujkić's sole international goal makes him statistically Bosnia's most prolific centre-back at this tournament — not bad for a defender.

players.name: Dennis Hadžikadunić
players.bio: Born in Malmö to Bosnian parents, Hadžikadunić represents the diaspora generation powering Bosnia's revival. A composed ball-playing defender, he joined Italian side Sampdoria after impressing across Europe. A World Cup debutant with 32 caps already banked.
players.facts: Hadžikadunić was born in Sweden but chose Bosnia over the Blagult — one of several Swedish-born players in this squad.

players.name: Tarik Muharemović
players.bio: Born in Ljubljana, this 23-year-old is one of Bosnia's most exciting defensive prospects. Muharemović brings composure and technical quality from his time at Sassuolo and represents the future of the Zmajevi backline at his debut World Cup.
players.facts: Born in Slovenia, raised with Bosnian pride — Muharemović is one of three different countries of birth among Bosnia's centre-backs alone.

players.name: Sead Kolašinac
players.bio: The tank of Karlsruhe turned Bosnian warrior. Kolašinac has been a fan favourite everywhere he's played — Arsenal, Marseille, Schalke and now Atalanta. Relentless, physical, and utterly committed, he brings 65 caps of hard-won experience to the left flank.
players.facts: Kolašinac once fought off knife-wielding attackers to defend teammate Mesut Özil in London in 2019 — genuinely one of football's hardest men.

players.name: Benjamin Tahirović
players.bio: Born in Spånga, Sweden, this defensive midfield engine chose the Dragons over the Blagult. At just 23, Tahirović has made 28 appearances for Bosnia and brings energy, discipline and a winning mentality honed at Brøndby. A future captain in the making.
players.facts: Tahirović was born on 03/03/2003 — his birthdate is a perfect numerical pattern that even his opponents might find hard to crack.

players.name: Amar Dedić
players.bio: The Austrian-born right-back who traded the Alps for Lisbon's glamour, earning a move to Benfica while still a teenager. Dedić is quick, attack-minded and technically refined. At 23, he arrives at his first World Cup as one of Europe's most promising full-backs.
players.facts: Dedić plays for Benfica — making him the highest-profile club representative in this Bosnian squad alongside a certain living legend.

players.name: Armin Gigović
players.bio: Another product of the Swedish-Bosnian pipeline, Gigović is a creative midfielder who combines technical flair with an eye for goal. Developed at Young Boys in Switzerland, he has grown into a reliable international with 20 caps before his 25th birthday.
players.facts: Gigović was born in Lund, Sweden — the same city that houses one of Scandinavia's oldest universities. Coincidence or genius? Probably both.

players.name: Samed Baždar
players.bio: The youngest forward in the squad, Baždar was born in Novi Pazar and is carving out a reputation at Jagiellonia Białystok in Poland. At just 22, his direct running and physical presence make him a handful for any defence. The future is bright.
players.facts: Baždar plays in Poland's top flight for Jagiellonia Białystok — a club whose name remains a tongue-twister challenge at every press conference.

players.name: Ermedin Demirović
players.bio: Hamburg-born, Bundesliga-proven, and carrying Bosnia's No.10 with pride. Demirović has been one of the Bundesliga's most dangerous forwards at Stuttgart, combining intelligent movement with clinical finishing. A player of genuine top-flight pedigree in his prime.
players.facts: Demirović scored 14 Bundesliga goals in the 2023/24 season for Augsburg, earning his big move to VfB Stuttgart — Bosnia's in-form attacking weapon.

players.name: Edin Džeko
players.bio: A living legend. Džeko is Bosnia's all-time top scorer with 73 goals in 148 caps — numbers that defy belief. At 40, the Sarajevo-born icon plays on at Schalke, defying time itself. This World Cup may be his final dance, and what a career to celebrate.
players.facts: Džeko has scored 73 international goals — more than Thierry Henry, Dennis Bergkamp, and most strikers you care to name. Quite the CV.

players.name: Mladen Jurkas
players.bio: At just 18, Jurkas is the youngest player in Bosnia's World Cup squad and hasn't yet earned a senior cap. The Borac Banja Luka goalkeeper represents the next generation of Bosnian talent. Soaking up this experience will be invaluable for his development.
players.facts: Born in 2007, Jurkas was only 18 when named in a World Cup squad — he was literally a toddler when Džeko scored his first international goal.

players.name: Ivan Bašić
players.bio: Born in Imotski, Croatia, Bašić chose Bosnia and has become a creative force in attacking midfield. Plying his trade with Astana in Kazakhstan, he's proof that ambition knows no geography. Seventeen caps in and hungry for a World Cup moment.
players.facts: Bašić plays club football in Kazakhstan — meaning he regularly travels further to an away game than most players fly for an international.

players.name: Ivan Šunjić
players.bio: A disciplined defensive midfielder from Zenica who has built his career across Europe, currently anchoring midfield for Pafos in Cyprus. Šunjić brings experience and composure to the engine room, offering the Zmajevi solidity when the game gets tough.
players.facts: Šunjić has played club football in Bosnia, Germany, Croatia and now Cyprus — a true journeyman collecting passport stamps as eagerly as caps.

players.name: Amar Memić
players.bio: Sarajevo-born and Czech-league-tested, Memić has developed into a reliable right-sided midfielder at Viktoria Plzeň. A hardworking, direct presence on the flank, his 13 caps show growing trust from the coaching staff ahead of his World Cup debut.
players.facts: Memić plays for Viktoria Plzeň — a club with genuine Champions League pedigree, which has sharpened his game considerably.

players.name: Amir Hadžiahmetović
players.bio: Born in Nexø, Denmark — yes, really — Hadžiahmetović is living proof that Bosnia's talent pool is truly international. The Hull City midfielder brings combativeness and craft in equal measure, with 36 caps confirming his place as a trusted international regular.
players.facts: Hadžiahmetović was born in Nexø, a small town on the Danish island of Bornholm — possibly the most remote birthplace in this entire squad.

players.name: Dženis Burnić
players.bio: Born in Hamm, Germany, Burnić is a tenacious defensive midfielder who has made his name in the 2. Bundesliga with Karlsruher SC. Twenty caps for Bosnia reflect his steady rise. A worker who wins the ball and makes space for more creative teammates.
players.facts: Burnić and Kolašinac are both German-born Bosnians who chose the Dragons — proving Germany has been quietly developing Bosnia's squad for years.

players.name: Nikola Katić
players.bio: A Ljubuški-born defender who can operate across the backline, Katić has shown his goals threat with two international strikes in 17 caps — remarkable for a defender. Now at Schalke 04, he brings versatility and an unexpected eye for goal.
players.facts: Katić and Džeko are both at Schalke 04 — so the legend and the utility defender share a training pitch every week. Great dinner conversations guaranteed.

players.name: Kerim Alajbegović
players.bio: Born in Cologne in 2007, Alajbegović is one of European football's most exciting teenage talents, already earning a move to Red Bull Salzburg. With 10 caps and a World Cup at 18, the sky is genuinely the limit for Bosnia's brightest young forward.
players.facts: Alajbegović was born the same year the iPhone launched — and at 18 he's already at a World Cup. Some people really do have it all figured out early.

players.name: Esmir Bajraktarević
players.bio: Born in Appleton, Wisconsin, Bajraktarević is arguably the most uniquely-sourced player at this World Cup. The PSV Eindhoven midfielder chose Bosnia over the USA and has flourished in the Eredivisie. American-born, Bosnian-proud, and genuinely talented.
players.facts: Born in Appleton, Wisconsin, Bajraktarević turned down the USMNT to represent Bosnia — the most American-Bosnian transfer of allegiance imaginable.

players.name: Stjepan Radeljić
players.bio: A solid, dependable centre-back from Nova Bila, Radeljić has been a consistent figure in Croatian club football with Rijeka. Five caps suggest he's still working his way into the national picture, but World Cup experience could prove transformative.
players.facts: Radeljić plays his club football at Rijeka in Croatia — meaning he crosses into a neighbouring country just to go to work every day.

players.name: Martin Zlomislić
players.bio: Posušje-born goalkeeper and Rijeka's dependable No.2, Zlomislić has three senior caps to his name. A reliable backup option with solid club credentials, he arrives at the World Cup ready to support and challenge for his place between the posts.
players.facts: Zlomislić's name roughly translates to 'evil mind' in Bosnian/Croatian — which is either terrifying for strikers or deeply unfortunate for a goalkeeper.

players.name: Haris Tabaković
players.bio: Born in Grenchen, Switzerland, Tabakovic is a poacher's poacher — four goals in just 10 caps is an extraordinary return. The Borussia Mönchengladbach striker brings lethal penalty-box instincts and could be a surprise package at this World Cup.
players.facts: Tabakovic averages a goal every 2.5 caps internationally — a ratio that most strikers with 50+ appearances would kill for. Clinical barely covers it.

players.name: Arjan Malić
players.bio: Born in Jesenice, Slovenia, Malić is one of the youngest outfield players in Bosnia's squad at just 20. The Sturm Graz centre-back has already earned eight senior caps and represents exactly the kind of exciting defensive talent Bosnia is building around.
players.facts: Malić was born in Slovenia, plays in Austria, and represents Bosnia — at 20 he's already lived a genuinely pan-Balkan football existence.

players.name: Jovo Lukić
players.bio: A journeyman striker who has carved a career across the Balkans and Eastern Europe, Lukić now plays for Universitatea Cluj in Romania. Three caps without a goal suggest he's very much the squad depth option, but every World Cup squad needs a reliable professional.
players.facts: Lukić plays in Romania, was born in a Bosnian town called Šabac, and represents a third country internationally. European football geography is wild.

players.name: Ermin Mahmić
players.bio: Born in Wels, Austria, Mahmić is one of the freshest faces in the squad with just two senior caps. The 21-year-old attacking midfielder from Slovan Liberec is still finding his international feet but his inclusion signals real faith from the coaching staff.
players.facts: Mahmić is one of only two players in this squad born in Austria — testament to the vast Bosnian diaspora that has spread across Central Europe.
